Every person has a creative side, and it can be expressed in many ways: problem solving, original and innovative thinking, and artistically, to name a few. Describe how you express your creative side.  





Nicknaming me “Property Destroyer”, my mother perfectly captured the way I manifest my creativity—dismantling things in hopes of salvaging components for my next invention. My imagination runs wild as I reassemble the pieces into something new. So, rather than a destroyer, I see myself as an inventor.





As much as I tried to suppress my creative mind to avoid breaking more things, my unconscious mind continued to concoct my next invention. Sitting in AP Biology, I thought about how one might take advantage of hydrogen atoms’ adhesive properties. AP Physics had currents of air swirling around my brain as I pondered how to capitalize on their movements. At home, I saw a sole worn-out air purifier; “Property Destroyer” pounded on the door of her oppressive cell, begging to take a crack at improving its functionality.





Most filters are neither cost-efficient nor environmentally-friendly. It then hit me: instead of expensive, disposable filters, why not use water as a natural filter? Its adhesive properties would make for the perfect medium to collect dust particles. I found that this had scarcely been done before, and no designs were elegant or affordably manufactured.





Hence began my creative endeavor of inventing a water-based purifier. I ripped apart a small, broken humidifier I had and took out the mist atomizer and circuit board. Next, something had to pull out the air to accelerate the filtering; the mini fan I kept in my box of dismantled items was perfect. For the body of the purifier, I decided to connect a short plastic box–which collects water–to a long, clear one that makes the mist visible. 





Prototype after prototype, I added more components: new layers in the body of the purifier to enhance filtration, and even a color-changing LED bulb that illuminated the mist and turned it into a nightlight. Each time the water leaked or the fan stopped, I saw my failure not as a setback but as an opportunity for me to utilize creativity. My biggest gain was not the awards I received for my invention, but my rediscovery of my favorite way of exercising creativity.













































Think about an academic subject that inspires you. Describe how you have furthered this interest inside and/or outside of the classroom.





As I listened to UC Professor Maloy’s lecture about the wonders of molecular biology, I started to realize just how much I have yet to discover about the brilliant designs of nature.





Intrigued by the practical applications of molecular biology, I started interning at a biotechnology startup near my house that was developing a pharmaceutical testing apparatus called PAMPA Dissolution, a novel approach to testing drug permeability in the gastrointestinal tract using artificial membranes. 





Contrary to my expectations, I was not greeted with a warm introduction to the lab. Instead, a researcher provided me with seven different daunting studies on PAMPA.  Like a fish out of water, I found myself surrounded by a group of experienced researchers. I knew I had to catch up. I spent weeks going through all the papers, asking for clarifications whenever I visited their lab. 





I would spend hours staring intensively at PAMPA Dissolution, trying to figure out how all of its components worked. Never did I feel worn out, because what I was looking at was a piece of art to me, a beautiful product of engineering: it had two compartments, one dedicated to the dissolution of drugs and the other for permeation, both connected to a computer on which a graphing software automatically depicted the data. It was a breath of fresh air: as much as I have studied biology, I have encountered nothing close to this kind of novel technology within the walls of a classroom. 





After I finally understood how the instrument worked, I helped with the trials by testing a generic drug called telmisartan repeatedly, and I made alterations to the lipid content of the PAMPA membrane so the results would become more consistent. When we finally achieved satisfactory results, I wrote a research paper on PAMPA Dissolution and had it published in a peer-reviewed journal.





This experience opened my eyes to a world of biotechnology that I realized I had only seen a small sliver of. I could only imagine what more I can uncover at the UCs from classes with brilliant professors and peers, research opportunities, and clubs. 









































What have you done to make your school or your community a better place?





It pained me to see my mother struggle to prove her identity on her phone and at airports after shaving off part of her fingerprint while preparing dinner. I wondered, was it only her who had this issue?





Recalling my knowledge on how fingerprint patterns can degrade overtime with the loss of skin elasticity, I set out to interview the seniors in my community to determine whether they have had difficulties with fingerprint recognition. The problem was more widespread than I had presumed: senior members of my school faculty expressed annoyance over ATM machines failing repeatedly to recognize their fingerprints as they watched younger people withdraw money with ease; my diabetic relatives who have to poke the tips of the fingers to measure blood glucose held the same sentiment. 





Staggered by the prevalence of this issue, I joined Professor Hsia’s research team to develop a deep learning model for finger vein recognition. Finger veins are internal biometrics; they cannot be damaged or stolen without penetrating a person’s skin. It occurred to me that finger vein recognition would be much more convenient for the seniors in my community whose fingerprints have eroded with age. 





Spending hours with Professor Hsia’s team, I trained a finger vein recognition AI model, hoping to improve its accuracy so that companies, banks, and even airport security would trust it enough to adopt it. As I fed images of finger vein patterns to the AI, it would sometimes fail to correctly identify the person. But thinking of all the people around me who would benefit from this technology, I felt motivated to continue to adjust the parameters in our code.





I dreamed for finger vein recognition to be widely adopted, but I understood this cannot happen quickly. Therefore, I presented our research on finger vein recognition at an international conference. This was the next best thing to do: sharing technology with other scientists, and raising awareness that relying on fingerprints can be a burden to many individuals in our communities. At the UCs, I hope to continue to use science for the betterment of different communities.













































What would you say is your greatest talent or skill? How have you developed and demonstrated that talent over time?





I watched Anastasiia’s cheeks grow flushed as she clumsily tried to pronounce newly-learned words; I fretted over the struggles I imagined she would face communicating with her new communities in Germany, where she had been displaced due to the Russo-Ukrainian war.





It puzzled me. Anastasiia has a charming Ukrainian accent when speaking English–series becomes sayleese, these becomes dees. Yet, despite having a decent command of English, she hesitated to speak both during our tutoring sessions and in Germany, where she needed to speak English more than ever.





I knew then that I had to exercise my skill of looking at problems through different angles to find a way to build Anastasiia’s confidence. 





Was it the words I had been teaching her? Epitome, phenomenon, obsolete–these were the words that I was told suited Anastasiia’s English abilities. As I thought from her perspective, I realized that she did not have the privilege to speak with carefully wrought sentences; she had to be responsive in real life. But when I started teaching easier words, Anastasiia already knew them all.





Was it the mechanics of pronouncing these words? I researched the best ways to break words into syllables and demonstrate tongue movements, but the detailed instructions seemed to help little.





Maybe, I realized, the problem was less technical than emotional–a problem of embarrassment, not skill. Hence, I started having her teach me Ukrainian words. There I was, struggling to pronounce a simple farewell, or [proshchavaj] in phonetic notation. She laughed, and I laughed too, in a shared understanding of the intricacies of language learning, of how our lived experiences shaped the phonemes we were accustomed to. 





Today, solving problems from different perspectives has become my greatest skill. From Anastasiia, who spoke bravely thereafter, I learned that what we perceive as problems are often entirely different from what we ought to solve. As a student in the University of California school system, I would strive to continue exercising this skill to devise solutions that would not have occurred to me otherwise, whether it be solving problems during classes or challenges faced by the school community.





























The epitome of femininity is perhaps when a woman tenderly and delicately handles everything she touches with love and care. But, to my parents’ dismay, nothing left my hands unscathed when I was young thanks to my habit of dismantling. Hence, I earned the title of “Property Destroyer” from my mom, which, at the time, was anything but affectionate.

Each time I stealthily snuck off with my dad’s screwdriver set to take something apart, I envisioned myself a brilliant inventor who could reassemble seemingly trivial parts into breathtaking gadgets. A source of endless headaches for my parents, my habit of dismantling objects was really a manifestation of this ambitious dream to become an inventor. But with each failed trial and each scolding I received–deservedly so, especially the time I took apart a brand new, lustrously white toaster–I learned to give up on this dream.

However, my unconscious mind continued to drift towards my naive dream. As I sat listening to a molecular biology lecture, I inevitably thought about how one might take advantage of hydrogen atoms’ adhesive properties. Physics class had currents of air swirling around my brain as I made sense of how to capitalize on their movements. At home, I saw my family’s sole worn-out air purifier; “Property Destroyer” pounded on the door of her oppressive cell, begging to take a crack at improving its functionality. But I quieted that voice, patiently doing more research before breaking anything.

 The filters I found were neither cost-efficient nor environmentally friendly. It then hit me: instead of expensive, disposable filters, why not use water as a natural filter? Its adhesive properties would make for the perfect medium to collect dust particles. I quickly researched to see if this had been done before; it had, but the results were unsatisfying. None of the few designs I saw were elegant or could be manufactured affordably.

 

Thus, my endeavor of inventing a water-based purifier began. I ripped apart a small, broken humidifier I had and took out the mist atomizer and circuit board it was attached to.

 

Next, I would need something to pull out the air to speed up the process. I glanced at my closet door. Inside was a box of dismantled items, intended to be kept hidden from my sight—every sight of it only served as a brutal reminder of my unrealistic, childish dream. But today, it had something I needed. I opened the box, taking out a mini-fan that would be perfect for the job. For the physical structure of the air purifier, I decided to connect a short plastic box – which collects water – to a long, clear one that would make the mist visible.

 

With each prototype came new components and mistakes: new layers in the body of the purifier to make filtration more effective, a LED bulb that illuminated the mist into different colors and turned it into a nightlight. The water would leak or the fan would stop from improperly bonded wires. But this was exciting to me: I was just like the inventors and engineers who had come before me, creating perfect designs only after making a million tweaks.

Eventually, I reached a prototype I was content with and applied for a patent. My patent was granted, and I earned the judges’ approval at international invention fairs! While I pretend to be annoyed whenever my friends jokingly call me Ms. Thomas Edison, I cannot help but ignore the rush of fulfillment each time I hear it.

I wear my title of “Property Destroyer” proudly now. Innovation will always be a clumsy process filled with failed attempts and discarded pieces for me, but I know I can fearlessly confront the challenges ahead. And along the way, my feats of engineering may even improve the quality of life for others.

Draft 5:
“I am disappointed.” One of the people I cared most about shook his head as I approached him after the race. His words imprinted into my mind. 
 
I joined the swimming team in 7th grade to keep a childhood hobby alive. My teammates, however, had all started in elementary school and were all national and regional champions—much due to Coach Chang, who always held everyone to high standards. It was immediately clear how behind I was. However, Coach especially looked out for me, working overtime to instruct me on swimming techniques. In just a year, I rapidly improved, and Coach Chang felt I was ready for a regional competition. I was excited for my first competition; most of all, it was time to pay back my coach by doing well.
 
The competition pool was much larger than the one I usually trained at. Seeing the other athletes made my legs quiver: they were all taller, stronger, fitter than me. The pressure was palpable. With the blow of a whistle, I was stunned on land; with those first few seconds of delay, I’d completely ruined my first-ever competition. Getting out of the pool and seeing Coach standing there with his arms crossed over his chest and legs firmly planted on the ground sent shivers down my spine; his shoulders were tense, and his gaze was piercing. That is when Coach told me the words I would never forget. 
 
I did not return to training the next day. My phone was filled with unread messages from my swim mates asking where I’d been; I sealed myself in my room, staring into the abyss as pieces of the competition played over in my head. My love for swimming retreated as those three words echoed in my brain. Suddenly, my phone buzzed: it was Coach. I ended the call. A few seconds later, Coach called again. I reluctantly picked up, and there were no harsh words, no criticisms. Instead, he told me his story.

He had trained since he was a toddler, always putting swimming before everything else. But one day, he had a blowout argument with his father. Only later did he realize what an advantage the extreme training, harsh words, and isolation his father put him through were. However, even if Coach wanted to swim again, his body had already passed its prime. Coach said, “Giving up is never an option if you want to achieve something great; by the time you start regretting, it might be too late.” His words were an epiphany: I no longer wanted to be the quitter I was.
 
The next day, I woke up at five and went straight to training. I spent approximately five hours each day in the pool. For a year, no matter how sore my muscles were, how cold the weather was, or how infected my pink eye was, my determination to train remained resolute. 

Finally, the annual swimming competition came again. This time, I had experience, confidence, and skills built over two years. With the blow of a whistle, I leaped into the bone-shriveling water. My mind was focused: there was only the water and me. With the blink of an eye, I touched the finishing wall. I looked around me, and seeing no one, I knew I had finished first in my group. When I looked at the timer and saw I had finally beaten my personal best record by almost five seconds, I leaped out of the water with tears in my eyes and gave Coach a big hug. Words can't describe how grateful I was to be able to return the dedication and patience Coach had shown me.
 
That was the second moment imprinted in my mind forever: Coach Chang’s look of pride as I climbed out of the pool. It was the greatest proof of the power of perseverance, something I won’t ever let go of.

Prompt: The lessons we take from obstacles we encounter can be fundamental to later success. Recount a time when you faced a challenge, setback, or failure. How did it affect you, and what did you learn from the experience?

Reason: I used to be a swimmer starting from 7th grade; compared to others I started quite late as they all started in their elementary years. Every day I trained for 1 hour in the morning from 6-7 and 2 hours at night from 5-7, however, in my first and second competitions my scores were not ideal.


“I am disappointed with you,” my coach shook his head and told me right after I finished the swimming competition; those were the words forever imprinted in my mind. What would you do if you constantly failed to meet the expectation of someone who cared about you so much? Would you have given up, or would you attempt to satisfy them? These were the questions I grappled with when I first joined the swimming them. The swimming team I joined was filled with national and regional champions, and my coach always held high expectations for everyone, including me. I joined the team at the start of my seventh-grade year, wanting to continue a childhood hobby of mine while keeping me healthy. Entering a swimming team at that age is considered pretty late for an athlete, as most of my teammates have been swimming since elementary school. That means I must put in extra work to catch up with my peers. I would say the heart and soul of our team have to be my coach Chang; everyone on the team has very close relations with the coach, and he is like a second father to us, particularly to me.  From the moment I first met my coach, I knew that he was someone who would push me to be my best and help me achieve my goals. He is a dedicated individual who understands you and is always there when you needed. The effort my coach put especially into compelling me to achieve better is immeasurable; however, there was a time when I constantly failed his expectation and made the coach I look up to disappointed with me.

In the beginning, I rapidly improved in every swimming style; I swam faster and faster with every periodical test until one month, all improvements ceased as if I had reached the peak of my capability. I recall it being the start of my eighth-grade year, my coach felt I was ready for a regional competition as I have been training for one year. I was very excited the night before the competition since it was my first ever official competition, and more importantly, it was my time to pay back my coach’s hard work by achieving a good score. The swimming pool where the competition was hosted was much larger than the one I trained at; besides the pool, many athletes like me were warming up and preparing for the battle. When I saw all these trained athletes who were taller, stronger, and fitter than me, I started to panic; making things worst, I was the first in our team to compete, which made me a deciding factor in my team’s morale. The immense pressure made me completely break down; wanting to quit immediately, I still dragged myself onto the swimming block trembling with anxiety. With the blow of a whistle, the competitors beside me all leaped into the pool, leaving me stunned on land. For just that few seconds of delay, I had completely ruined my first-ever competition. When I got out of the pool, in the distance, I saw my coach standing there with his arms crossed over his chest and his leg firmly planted on the ground; his shoulders were tense, and in his gaze, I could sense his disappointment. I walked up to my coach, knowing that not only have I let myself down, I had also let my coach down; I had been training an entire just for this moment, but I messed it up. That is when my coach told me the words I would never forget. For the next few weeks, I did not return back for training: I was devastated. I was so traumatized by the event that I thought of quitting swimming forever. It was until one day that Coach Chang showed up at my house unnotified. He sat down next to me and told me a story about a boy who was born to a swimming coach father who raised the boy in the swimming pool. The father has always dreamt of his boy growing up to become a national swimming athlete that could complete the dream that he never achieved. He pushed the boy to his maximum capacity; besides studying and swimming, he did not allow the boy to chase any of his own dreams. After seventeen years, the boy broke down. He had a massive argument with his father and gave up swimming forever. Now the boy is almost thirty-five, but ironically, he leads swimming teams for a living. Looking back, the boy realizes that all the extreme training, harsh words, and isolation his father puts him through is for him to achieve a better self. However, even if the boy wanted to swim again, his body had already passed its prime period. Coach Chang told me, “Giving up is never an option if you want to achieve something great; by the time you start to regret your choices, it might be too late.” These words from my coach gave me an epiphany. 

The next day I woke up early at five and joined the training, and as soon as I finished school, I went straight to the swimming pool. For a year, I trained tirelessly without giving myself any breaks. Finally, the annual swimming competition came again, and without hesitation, I signed up. This time I have experience, confidence, and the skills I have built up over the past year. With the blow of a whistle, I leaped into the bone-shriveling water; however, this time, my mind was blank, I wasn’t distracted by anything instead, I just focused on pushing my legs and arms. With what to be thought of as a blink of an eye, I’ve touched the finishing walls. Seven battles in a row we lost. Although each game lasted only about 2 minutes, the self-imposed pressure and self-blame that accompanied each defeat added up exponentially. As the team leader, I felt as if the weight of the world rested on my shoulders. Ironically, I told myself not to concede failure or express my emotions so easily as to maintain a facade of composure, providing guidance and support to my teammates when all I wanted was a moment to vent my own frustrations. As frustrations and blaming started spreading across team members, I was irritated and angry, believing that we should compose ourselves instead of showing weakness. 
These suppressing emotions make me feel as fragile as a glass structure on the verge of shattering. As I went back home, tears went down my eyes. I was no longer able to digest the mood built up with the accumulated frustration. I began to regret the design choices I made for the robot, and I agonized over my lack of nuanced understanding of the programming. I was consumed by anger, self-blame, and frustration. This state of mind lingered for approximately two weeks, and I frequently carried it as a heavy burden.
Amidst moments of despair, my English tutor’s words come to my mind. She advised me to embrace “let it be.” and always envision the worst-case scenario as a beginning. This perspective reminds me that while I cannot change the past, I have the power to shape my response to it. Situations are not always favorable but each advert experience counts and is invaluable. More importantly, positioning myself for the worst-case scenario encourages an ongoing journey of self-discovery and growth. This reminds me of the badminton court. At the moment of losing the game, I let go of the win-and-loss mindset and think about strategy to survive. The worst scenario is just that we lose everything. That’s it! I have nothing to lose already. Surrender to a situation does not mean giving up. That is when a new path and possibility appear. 
The next competition, the TAS Signature Event, came quickly in December. However, I acquired a stronger and more determined mentality from the previous competition. No longer did I blame myself when we lost the game. Making mistakes did not occupy my mind.     Instead, Accepting those previous failures allows me to improve myself and my strategy as a catalyst. I became brave, starting to develop diverse unconstrained small designs. I became confident because I told myself that there was nothing to lose.  
Setbacks like the one I encountered with the Robotics Competition are formidable trials. But I embrace the notion that life unfolds like an enthralling novel, with its exposition, rising action, climax, falling action, and resolution. Everything will ultimately pass. Even in times of roughs, we can keep pushing forward toward the climax. Even during the climax, we will eventually return to the very beginning. While the result is disheartening, “let it be,” along with the practice of considering the worst situation serves as a growing catalyst that induces a period of healing, change, and improvement. Setbacks are temporary and inevitable in life, but my response to them can shape my journey.  I now believe that this is not an ending that defines me but rather a transition toward the next stage. It is a stepping stone toward a future defined by resilience and persistence, allowing me to surmount unprecedented challenges next.

The definition of a captain/leader has always been a question for me. When I joined the badminton team in primary school, the captain was chosen based on being the top player. In junior high, the Acappella club I attended was led by the oldest student. In high school, the Robotics team I joined had a captain with the most experience. Every place defines captaincy and leadership differently.
People often say that becoming a captain demonstrates leadership and that being a cadre indicates strong leadership abilities. However, I often question these notions because I have seen many cadres and captains who lack the so-called leadership skills and obtain their positions through parental intervention and extensive connections. Initially, I couldn't understand the relationship between a leader and a captain until I joined the Robotics Team, an experience that guided me to comprehend what it means to be a leader and a captain.
As a robot team captain, I always repeat these words, trying to push my team members and keep them on the right track. "What are you doing?" "We're still far from done." "This air valve should not be placed here because it will get stuck directly on this wheel."
In the beginning, none of us knew each other well. We simply divided the work, engaged in discussions, and worked hard together. Although we weren't very familiar with the parts and the competition process, the enthusiasm of our team members drove us to put in our best efforts from the start. Everyone was enthusiastic, like machines with boundless energy. The only break we took was during lunchtime. I genuinely enjoyed this atmosphere because it exemplified everyone's loyalty to Robotics.
However, as the machines became more complex and the small parts more challenging to assemble, a very small assembly error may cause the entire device to need to be reassembled. The most memorable instance was when we were assembling the intake mechanism for catching discs. During the construction of our first-generation robot, we had to modify the intake's length multiple times, adjusting the spacing of the wheels more than ten times. When we were on the verge of completing the assembly, we discovered that the supporting C-Channel was installed upside down. The feeling of assembling and disassembling repeatedly was despairing. Every time we fixed one problem, other details were often forgotten. Just working on the intake alone would take over 15 hours.
When my teammates felt that our machine was almost complete, I responded directly, saying, "It's still early!" and started pointing out the problems with our machine. My intuition told me that as a team captain, if I gave up, the entire team would lose its momentum. This was an unacceptable fact, and I knew I couldn't falter. It was my responsibility to supervise my teammates' progress and ensure that the construction of the robot was on the right track. This drove me to strive for perfection.
Gradually, I noticed that everyone's enthusiasm waned when we had to dismantle the original machine to build a new generation, and when we repeatedly built and dismantled, and when I pointed out areas for improvement. By the time we reached the stage of building the third and largest robot, with a completely different design for the intake and endgame structure, the team's progression had almost come to a halt.
At that point, I began to question the responsibilities of a team captain. I was perplexed and wondered why everyone had lost their initial motivation for joining the robotics team. The entire team was filled with despair and silence.
The season had been ongoing for a long time, with classes from morning until afternoon, leaving everyone exhausted. The section I was in charge of remained unfinished. Although I truly wanted to continue working without wasting any time, I noticed that our team had become demoralized. Some teammates were sighing repeatedly.
In the past, it was common for me to directly assist a teammate, complete the device they were in charge of, or provide advice. However, I decided to make a change. I invited all team members to come together and combine all the parts, even if they weren't perfect and had several issues. I was fully aware that the final result would require disassembling it again. I knew that our machine was still far from completion and that we shouldn't waste time on insignificant matters. However, I resolutely took the lead.
When all the parts were assembled, we witnessed the creation of a large and fully functional machine. As I started the machine, it successfully received and automatically shot the disc toward the goal. As my teammate operated the robot, it obediently followed their control, sucking in the disc and shooting it into the goal. As the team leader, I had an epiphany. At that moment, all of us cheered. We watched, manipulated, and played with the machine for over an hour. The difference, however, was the change in the team's atmosphere. There was no longer silence or repeated lamentations. Instead, every teammate felt a renewed sense of hope. That hour changed everything. In the subsequent competitions, our progress got back on track. It wasn't because we had a new design or achieved immediate success, but because our team's atmosphere had changed, motivating everyone to work towards our goals.
Looking back on it now, I used to firmly believe that as a team leader, it was crucial to work consistently and continuously to push the members' progress. I believed that guiding the team to work in unison and supervising each member's tasks were the defining qualities of a "true" leader. However, through my experience in the Robotics Team, I have come to realize that under prolonged pressure, teammates may lose sight of the possibility of success. It is essential to help each member perceive the importance of their work in an integrating project. Visualizing the possibility of achieving a successful final robot brings the team back together. The repetitive process of constructing the design becomes critical and meaningful. This characteristic is what shapes a true leader.

Her once vibrant countenance grew pale, and she lost all appetite, ushering in a difficult journey for our family. It was devastating and overwhelming for me to see my mom, the strong pillar of my family on whom my brother and I could always fall back, losing all her hair and becoming quite frail. Furthermore, due to my mother’s condition, my father had to accompany her to the hospital constantly; my brother and I had to be self-reliant and sensible and began to shoulder full responsibility for housework while maintaining our schooling, wanting to support our mother in any way we could, even if it was the slightest. In fact, I wasn’t always the obedient child like my brother growing up; I was the troublemaker whom my parents worried about all the time. I often argued with them over insignificant things, thinking they were grumpy adults who could never take my opinions into account. Yet, my mother’s diagnosis made me reevaluate my attitude and behaviors toward them, especially toward my mom, as it made me comprehend what grownups mean when they say, “Life is short; cherish everyone around you and don’t take their existence for granted.” Despite it being challenging and devastating for me and my family, I did not lose hope for my mother's situation and the treatments she was going through, believing she was a strong woman who would eventually overcome this drawback one day.

Every time she comes back, I will again ask her how the examination went, and like most of the times where she responds that everything is fine, it seems like a stone has dropped from my heart. Indeed, my mother's time fighting over cancer has left an indelible mark on my family as well as on me myself. In the past, it was usually my mother who took care of me no matter what, but her health condition had a significant impact on my personal growth and prompted a significant transformation in my role. I have transitioned into a young “adult” who now has the ability to independently manage my responsibilities and affairs, taking on the role of not only looking after myself but also tending to our family’s needs and obligations.

In the end, while our family was finally able to return to our dedication to regular volunteering activities, I began to appreciate more the small things around me that I used to take for granted, which made me discover the joy of helping people and planted a seed of passion for nature, motivating me to partake in numerous voluntary activities and take on initiatives to make positive impacts in my community and beyond.

1. Reflect on a social or community issue that deeply resonates with you. Why is it important to you, and how have you been involved in addressing or raising awareness about it?
“Joseph is smart”
“Nick is rich”
“York got more A than I did”

I am living in a city, where we compare everything, whether material possessions or academic performance. From a young age, I was taught to measure success against these external markers, creating a perpetual cycle of comparison that leads to feelings of inadequacy and unworthiness. 

The issue is particularly significant to me because I’ve compared everything I learned to my sisters, relatives, and close friends. It’s almost as if this habit of comparison is a reflex, shaping our self-worth based on how we stack up against others. 

One particular example was when I struggled with not being at the top of the class. Seeing “York got more A’s than I did” became more than just a statement, I became jealous and started my mental comparison with him in every single quiz and assignment. This obsession led me to seek shortcuts to achieve higher grades, often relying on searching for answers online or even copying others’ work into my project, all driven by the single desire to “get more A than he did”. 

I won this duel.

Yet, “So what?” 

Looking back, I was lost. Apart from chasing York, I haven’t grown substantially. Constantly fulfilling requirements and others’ expectations, I felt like I was a shadow, always trailing behind others - lack of creation, direction, and motivation. I was lost. 

Comparison is detrimental to a healthy self-worth. This taught me a valuable lesson: The importance of focusing on personal growth and development rather than perpetually measuring myself against others because we are all different. I learned to always set a goal for myself, whether it is as modest as completing homework every day or as ambitious as being a millionaire. As I witness that I’ve done something better than the previous me, the compulsion to compare with others fades away. I have learned to look beyond external achievement and focus on inner growth. 

It seems to me that comparison exists as a human nature and it would not be easy to eliminate.  However, I believe a growth model is a mentality worth promoting. I have planted a seed in myself for a future thriving and spreading.

What would you say is your greatest talent or skill? How have you developed and demonstrated that talent over time?
"I am disappointed." Right after the race, what was waiting for me was a shaking head and this line that was then imprinted on my mind.
I joined the swimming team in seventh grade to keep a childhood hobby alive. Fortunately, my coach especially looked out for me and was willing to work overtime to instruct me on swimming techniques. In just a year, I rapidly improved, and my coach felt I was ready for a regional competition. I was excited for my first competition; most of all, it was time to pay back my coach by doing well.
On the day of the competition, the pressure was palpable—I ruined my first-ever competition completely. Seeing my coach with his arms crossed over his chest sent shivers down my spine. That was also when he told me the words I would never forget.
However, my determination and commitment remained strong. The next day, I was the first to arrive at the pool. Training started at six in the morning. 2,000 meters to go, then a quick shower, and it was off to school. Back in the pool at five, the afternoon training schedule features an additional 3,000 meters. This became my routine for the year, no matter how sore my muscles were, how cold the weather was, or how infected my pink eye was.
Finally, the annual swimming competition came again. With the blow of a whistle, I leaped into the water. My mind was focused. With the blink of an eye, I touched the finishing wall. I looked toward the timer—I had just beaten my personal best record by almost five seconds. I leaped out of the water with tears in my eyes and gave my coach a big hug.
Swimming has taught me that the difference between success and failure is defined by the ability to turn obstacles into motivation and persist in what I do. With one-part optimism and one-part perseverance, my ability to reshape my adversities has become core to my identity and gives me the courage to face challenges and unknowns in the future.

What have you done to make your school or your community a better place?
As the child of an elementary school teacher, I have always wanted to have a chance to teach others to feel the responsibility students will never have.
My dream finally came true in eighth grade when I had the opportunity to teach English to elementary children from local Taiwanese schools in a book reading club. In the beginning, the students barely understood the stories and the language, but slowly, the confused look on their faces faded as we read more books and their vocabulary expanded. This experience made me realize the fun of teaching does not come from the privilege of having my students listen to or follow my words; instead, it comes from the fulfilling smile on their faces when they are able to follow along with the stories.
Back in school, I volunteered as a student assistant in the fourth-grade class and continued to help others with their English. Yet, it was not that easy this time. A Japanese girl in the class was having a hard time keeping up with the lessons and refused to communicate with me at first when I tried to help out. I then devised a plan for striking up a conversation with her by bringing up her favorite animal. With my successful attempt, I began to teach her English after school and tried my best to translate course materials into Japanese with the help of technology. Admittedly, my Japanese translations were often far from perfect; however, I saw the improvement in her English, where she became able to understand in class and hold conversations with others.
Outside of school, I joined a peer tutoring program for lower-income students and was paired with a local high school student who had a clear goal to pass the intermediate-level Taiwanese General English Proficiency Test. We broke down each section of the test to identify concepts that she was unfamiliar with, and she eventually passed the test. Meanwhile, I came to realize the importance of understanding the condition that people are in through communication to better help them adapt to various situations and achieve their goals.

What have you done to make your school or your community a better place? 
Things to consider: Think of community as a term that can encompass a group, team or a place like your high school, hometown or home. You can define community as you see fit, just make sure you talk about your role in that community. Was there a problem that you wanted to fix in your community?
Why were you inspired to act? What did you learn from your effort? How did your actions benefit others, the wider community or both? Did you work alone or with others to initiate change in your community?
As a child to an elementary school teacher, I have always dreamt of having a chance to teach others to feel the authority students will never have.
In eighth grade, I had my first chance to teach in my local book reading club, where I read English stories to teach elementary children from local Taiwanese schools. In the beginning, the students barely understood the stories as most of them hadn’t learned English, but slowly, with more books we read, their vocabulary expanded, and the confused look on their faces faded. This first successful teaching experience made me realize the fun of teaching does not come from the sense of power, instead, it sprouts from the fulfilling smile of the students when they finally understand a concept.
At school, I volunteered as a student assistant for the fourth-grade class, primarily focusing on students falling behind. I vividly remember a student named Mika. She is viewed as a troubled kid who always falls asleep during class, and even the teacher has given up on her. At first, it was hard to start a conversation with her, but slowly, by initiating conversations about her favorite animal, she explained she sleeps because she can’t understand the teacher. With this in mind, I translated course materials into Japanese (her first language) and taught her English after school. Mika rapidly improved, and at the end of the year, her grades for all subjects improved by ten percent. 
In the summer of eleventh grade, I joined a program initiated by the Xin Chuan Association focusing on educating English for lower-income students around Taiwan. Unlike before, I was paired with a sophomore named Luna from a local high school. Although Luna’s family conditions can’t allow her to study in English cram schools, she has clear ideas of what she wants to gain from the class, and she also expresses her goal to pass the intermediate-level Taiwanese General English Proficiency Test (GEPT). To help Luna achieve her dreams, I constructed a custom course on the critical sections of the GEPT after days of studying for the test. Luna was my proudest student who, although did not have the resources but, with her hard work, was able to pass the GEPT. 
Teaching these local school students made me realize the joy in teaching, and I believe every one of them will have great achievements when they grow up.

text:
The University of Rochester motto of Meliora - or "ever better" - deeply integrates critical core values into all that we do. These core values of equity, leadership, integrity, openness, respect and accountability define not only who we are, but also who we hope to become. Please use the space below to highlight a time, creative idea or research experience when you put into action one or more of these characteristics in order to make yourself, your community or the world ever better. (250 word limit)





After several years of growing concern over the sheer volume of security vulnerabilities reported in web frameworks' update patches and GitHub repositories, I decided to investigate the problem myself. At Pioneer, I focused my research on XSS attacks, one of the most common online security exploitations; they occur when hackers insert malicious code into a server in order to steal or manipulate users' data. I found that, to prevent these attacks, web engineers should stop relying on third party-hosted files - a common practice in the software industry- and should invest more in training beginner developers to implement basic XSS defense, which is easy in theory, but becomes more difficult as the size of applications increases.





In web engineering, "integrity" means that a file is the same when it arrives as when it was sent. This virtual "integrity" is integral to user trust in an application because it ensures the legitimacy of user data. What my research found was that integrity was also key to equity: security exploitations, minor or major alike, disproportionately harm under-resourced groups who lack access to security software and expert guidance on how to implement security procedures, such as enforcing HTTPS. The solution to XSS attacks is to invest in more server space and developer training: in other words, investing in integrity to increase equity. Protections to the Internet's transmission protocols, designed to prevent XSS attacks, can allow more people to browse the internet safely, without the risk of their data or machines becoming corrupted.

Share a time when you were most proud of yourself either as a role model or when you displayed your leadership. What specific skills did you contribute to the experience? How did others rely on you for guidance? What did you learn about yourself during this time? (120 words)





I'm proud to have become a leader in the Stack Overflow community. Having received valuable guidance on Stack Overflow throughout my own programming journey, during the past few summers, I spent an hour daily answering questions regarding the web-development framework Django. My answers eventually received 138K views, putting me in the top 0.3% upvoted for the entire platform in 2020. Original posters would often follow up, with conversations lasting from 15 minutes to two days. From this experience, I recognized my strong motivation to help new programmers as others helped me. I found my talent for simplifying complicated solutions: instead of throwing out abstract concepts and jargon, I incorporated examples, detailed explanations, and external references for self-directed learners.

Tell us why you decided to apply to the University of Wisconsin-Madison. In addition, please include why you are interested in studying the major(s) you have selected. If you selected undecided please describe your areas of possible academic interest. (you may enter up to 650 words). *
507 words
From my freshman year onward, the world of computers became an enticing ocean, compelling me to dive deeper and deeper. I began my exploration of computer networks by setting up a server on the video game "Minecraft." This was a moment that fully engaged me and made me lose track of time.

The moment I decided to major in computer science was when I wanted to create a server for my friends to play together. However, it turned out to be more complex than I initially thought. It involved tasks like configuring port settings and managing dynamic IP address translation. The University of Wisconsin-Madison offers a wealth of courses that align with my desire to delve deeper into understanding how the Internet interacts and connects securely, without compromising personal information. Some of these courses include COMP SCI 640 — Introduction to Computer Networks, COMP SCI 642 — Introduction to Information Security, COMP SCI/​E C E 707 — Mobile and Wireless Networking, COMP SCI 740 — Advanced Computer Networks, and COMP SCI 763 — Security and Privacy for Data Science. Among these, my favorite course is COMP SCI/​E C E 782 — Advanced Computer Security and Privacy. This course focuses on security and privacy issues in software, networks, and hardware systems, which are closely related to networking, security, and modern machine learning. As someone who has been intrigued by setting up servers and configuring network settings, I understand the importance of networking security and privacy. These courses will enable me to explore the theoretical aspects of these topics.

On the recreational side of things, I am drawn to the UW-Madison Symphony Orchestra. Music has been an integral part of my life since I was four years old. As a viola player, I serve as a compromiser, influencing the tonal colors of the pieces. The role of the viola holds a special place in my heart. This orchestra provides me with the opportunity to continue pursuing my long-standing passion alongside my major. One particular memory that vividly reinforces this truth is from an orchestra summer camp in the US in 2022: BUTI. It was there that I discovered a shared passion and an unspoken musical philosophy with many others. It felt as though we were engaging in a conversation without words, conveying our deepest emotions, thoughts, and aspirations through the universal language of music. I have found a similar environment at UW-Madison, where many contemporary improvisation players come together. I am eager to continue developing various styles and forms of musical performances with my peers. 

I believe that UW-Madison University has the qualities to enhance my journey in both engineering and music. Its incredible opportunities with Computer Science and its distinct music performance would give me access to stand at the front of the networking world accompanied by my favorite humanity activity.

“Ah! So this is the root of the problem!” I exclaimed to Mom with a disassembled motherboard, hard drive, and graphic card lying around me.

Just like that, my first encounter with engineering came when I was in eighth grade with my attempt to fix my mom’s laptop. Throughout that week, my screen was constantly filled with YouTube videos that introduced complex hardware functions inside computers. Day and night, I constantly disassembled and reassembled the device, digging into the root cause. In fact, it didn’t take me long to figure out that the device malfunction was due to a dead hard disk drive (HDD). However, I was not satisfied with simply replacing the old HDD, especially after learning the features of a solid-state drive (SSD)—a more robust and effective type of storage component—from one of the online tutorials. To carry out my intended upgrade, I investigated if an SSD could be used with an HDD adapter and eventually bought an SSD-to-HDD converter, leading to a “life-saving” moment for my mom’s laptop while igniting my passion for hands-on problem-solving as an engineer.

Yet, growing up in the high-tech center of Taiwan, I happened never to get enough of innovative technology and development. With most of my relatives being engineers in the semiconductor industry, my interest in the field of advanced nanotechnology that we use in indispensable digital devices was further fostered when work was once again brought to the dinner table or another family session. Meanwhile, my curiosity about the applications of electrical equipment motivated me to take Advanced Placement courses in physics and computing to better understand the field of electrical engineering. Moreover, to get a better view of the real-world applications of integrated circuits, I took up the opportunity to intern at a spectral chip company. During my time there, I dealt with sophisticated electronics, such as the Pleroma Mini Spectrometer and HG-1 Mercury Argon Calibration Source, to identify poor-performing spectral chips by looking at whether their signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) level reaches the required number through running tests. Overall, this experience not only exposed me to the intricacies of handling chips, integrating them into electronic devices, and running simulations but also enlightened me on the vital role of computational tools in modern chip testing and design, furthering my understanding of the application of high-end chips and my passion for related fields.

At UW-Madison, with my aspiration to make advancements in sustainable chip design solutions for higher data transmission efficiency, I hope to drive into the studies of electrical engineering through major core courses, as well as prepare myself for a future career in the semiconductor industry with a solid foundation through advanced electives, including “ECE 445 Semiconductor Physics and Devices” and “ECE 549 Integrated Circuit Fabrication Laboratory.” In addition, to further facilitate my goal while putting the knowledge I will learn into practice, I wish to work under Professor Azadeh Davoodi to research the topic of explainable machine learning for better VLSI chip design. I am also interested in Assistant Professor Chirag Gupta’s research on optoelectronic devices due to my previous internship experience at the spectral chip company. I hope to gather more insight into how these optoelectronic devices could serve as the backbone of clean and green solutions and, from there, develop a theme for my “ECE 491 Senior Design Project” after further exploration of various topics related to sustainable design while adapting to the needs of solutions for innovative applications such as AI.

Beyond the academic setting, UW-Madison provides an extensive array of extracurricular student organizations that would enable me to persist in following the interests that shape my identity and perspectives. Through joining The Badger Herald as a newspaper editor for sports or science and involving myself in the Badgers Men's Swimming & Diving team, I am excited to continue my passion from high school to express myself while striding forward for the better.

Through these simulations, I gained insights into identifying poor-performing chips where their SNR level does not reach the required number, which was not only enlightening but also underscored the vital role of computational tools in modern chip testing and design. This internship experience not only exposed me to the intricacies of handling chips, integrating them into electronic devices, and running simulations but also allowed me to work with instruments and undergrad students in the field, furthering my understanding of the application of high-end chips and my passion for working with chips.

After college, I aspire to make achievements in the field of semiconductors and AI, where I can make advancements in chip designs that improve data transmission efficiency and find sustainable energy solutions in chip production; thus, benefiting other fields like the medical field by providing more accurate diagnoses and faster treatment. At UW-Madison, I applied for the electrical engineering major to accomplish this goal.

The reason for me to apply to UW-Madison is because of its research opportunities, advanced faculty, and diverse student organizations. First, I am intrigued by the variety of cutting-edge electrical engineering research facilities UW-Madison provides, for example, the Nanoscale Fabrication Center (NFC) and Nanoscale Imaging and Analysis Center (NIAC). Second I wish to work under Professor Azadeh Davoodi to research the topic of “Explainable machine learning for better VLSI chip design,” as I am eager to apprehend the knowledge of chip designs and facilitate my goal of developing more efficient chips. I am also interested in Assistant Professor Chirag Gupta’s research in Optoelectronic devices due to my experience as an intern in the spectral chip company, where I hope to gain more insight into how these optoelectronics devices can serve as the backbone of clean and green solutions to the market (as mentioned on their page). Third, UW-Madison has a variety of student clubs and organizations that allow me to pursue my interests outside of the classroom. As an athlete in the badminton and swimming team in high school, I am excited to see the variety of athletic facilities that UW-Madison provides, for example, the Nicholas Recreation Center, where there is an Olympic-size pool that I am eager to train in with the Badger’s Men's Swimming & Diving team.

I am excited to leverage this experience and align it with UW-Madison’s unique resources to further my studies and contribute to the university's renowned Electrical Engineering program. Thank you for your time and consideration.
